findings.title: nopriv AJAX handler pagelayer_givecss - Informational / By Design
findings.description: The nopriv handler pagelayer_givecss serves static CSS assets (editor and frontend styles). The handler calls givecss.php which: (1) accepts an optional comma-separated 'give' parameter, (2) validates every requested filename against a hardcoded whitelist of known CSS files, and (3) only reads files from the plugin's own css/ directory. No path traversal is possible because file lookups use in_array() against the whitelist. There is no nonce on this endpoint but none is needed since it only serves static public CSS.
findings.exploitability: Not exploitable. Whitelist validation prevents directory traversal. All served files are intentionally public.
findings.recommendation: No action needed. The implementation is correct by design for a public CSS delivery endpoint.

findings.title: nopriv AJAX handler pagelayer_contact_submit - Email Header Injection via custom template headers
findings.description: The pagelayer_contact_submit handler is accessible to unauthenticated users and is protected only by a nonce (pagelayer_global) embeddable in the page. User-supplied form field values are sanitized with sanitize_text_field() and CRLF-stripped via pagelayer_esc_crlf() before being substituted into the email body and subject. However, the 'additional headers' value ($additional_headers) is fetched from the pagelayer_cf_headers WordPress option (admin-configured) and interpolated directly into $headers without CRLF stripping before substitution of sanitized user vars. This means an admin misconfiguration could expose header injection, but a regular unauthenticated visitor cannot inject new headers because their input is CRLF-stripped before substitution. The subject line does receive sanitize_text_field() which strips tags but does not prevent SMTP header injection if a custom template 'cont_subject' contains user-controlled vars—however user data is CRLF-stripped before that substitution. Overall risk is low for a correctly configured site, moderate for sites with attacker-controlled options (requires admin compromise).
findings.exploitability: Low for unauthenticated users: user data is CRLF-escaped before header interpolation. Risk escalates if an attacker controls admin options (separate admin compromise required). The global nonce is embedded in any page that renders the contact form, making CSRF a minor concern for form replay but not for header injection.
findings.recommendation: Apply wp_strip_all_tags() and CRLF stripping to any admin-configured header/subject templates before passing to wp_mail(). Consider using wp_mail()'s structured $headers array instead of a raw string built from options.

findings.title: nopriv AJAX handler pagelayer_login_submit - Username enumeration via wp_signon error messages
findings.description: The pagelayer_login_submit handler is accessible to unauthenticated users protected by the pagelayer_global nonce. Credentials (username, password, remember_me) are passed directly to wp_signon() without prior sanitization. WordPress's built-in wp_signon() handles actual authentication safely. However, the error messages from $user->get_error_message() are returned verbatim to the client as JSON. WordPress's default wp_signon() error messages distinguish 'unknown username' from 'incorrect password' in older configurations, enabling username enumeration. Additionally, there is no rate limiting implemented in this handler, enabling credential stuffing. The logout_url and login_url parameters are sanitized with sanitize_url().
findings.exploitability: An unauthenticated attacker can send requests to this endpoint (the nonce is obtainable from any page with the login widget). Username enumeration is possible if WP is configured to reveal distinct login errors. Credential stuffing is unconstrained at the plugin level.
findings.recommendation: Normalize error messages to a single generic string (e.g., 'Invalid username or password') regardless of error code. Implement rate limiting (e.g., use WP's built-in transient-based lockout or a dedicated plugin). Consider adding wp_check_password_strength() guidance on the client side rather than exposing server-side errors.

findings.title: nopriv AJAX handler pagelayer_infinite_posts - Unvalidated atts array passed to shortcode renderer
findings.description: The pagelayer_infinite_posts handler is accessible to unauthenticated users protected only by the pagelayer_global nonce (obtainable from any page using the posts widget). The entire $_REQUEST['data']['atts'] array is passed unsanitized to get_comment_delimited_block_content() and then to pagelayer_the_content(). There is no allowlist or sanitization of individual attribute values before they are processed as shortcode attributes. Depending on what pagelayer_the_content() and the pl_posts/pl_archive_posts shortcode handlers do with these attributes, this could enable: (1) stored-XSS if any attribute value is reflected into HTML output without escaping, (2) SQL injection if attributes affect WP_Query arguments without sanitization. The tag parameter is validated against a two-value allowlist ('pl_posts' or 'pl_archive_posts').
findings.exploitability: Requires knowledge of the nonce (obtainable from page source) and knowledge of shortcode attributes accepted by the rendered shortcode. Impact depends on how downstream shortcode handlers sanitize individual attributes. This is a medium-severity attack surface requiring deeper shortcode handler review.
findings.recommendation: Sanitize each expected attribute individually before passing to the shortcode renderer. Maintain an allowlist of valid attribute names and sanitize values by type (intval for integers, sanitize_text_field for strings, esc_url for URLs). Do not pass raw $_REQUEST arrays to content renderers.

findings.title: False Positive: exec() comment in functions.php - No actual exec() call present
findings.description: The automated scan flagged line 181 of functions.php due to the text 'exec()' appearing in a code comment. The actual code is the pagelayer_inputsec() function which uses addslashes() and str_replace() to escape backticks — specifically to prevent backtick command substitution if the string were ever passed to a shell. There is no exec(), system(), passthru(), shell_exec(), popen(), or proc_open() call anywhere in the main/ directory. The comment is a developer note explaining the defensive purpose of the backtick escaping.
findings.exploitability: Not applicable. No shell execution function is present.
findings.recommendation: No action needed. The security function pagelayer_inputsec() is appropriately defensive.

findings.title: Reflected XSS in editor iframe src via $_SERVER['QUERY_STRING'] - Requires authenticated editor access
findings.description: The live editor frame constructs an iframe src by directly concatenating $_SERVER['QUERY_STRING'] without escaping: `<iframe src="...'.$_SERVER['QUERY_STRING'].'..."`. This is rendered inside the Pagelayer live editor which is only accessible to users who pass the pagelayer_user_can_edit() check, which requires current_user_can($object->cap->edit_posts). An unauthenticated user cannot reach this code path. For authenticated editors, a specially crafted URL with characters like `"` in the query string could break out of the iframe src attribute and inject HTML/JavaScript. This constitutes a self-XSS/reflected-XSS requiring social engineering of an editor-role user.
findings.exploitability: Requires luring an authenticated editor-role (or higher) user to click a crafted URL. Since the victim must already have edit_posts capability, the privilege escalation impact is minimal. This could be chained in a targeted attack against a specific editor.
findings.recommendation: Escape the query string with esc_attr() before embedding in the HTML attribute: `esc_attr($_SERVER['QUERY_STRING'])`.

findings.title: Intentional raw echo of admin-configured header/footer/body code - By Design
findings.description: The functions pagelayer_add_custom_head(), pagelayer_body_open(), and pagelayer_add_custom_footer() echo unescaped content from: (1) WordPress options (pagelayer_header_code, pagelayer_body_open_code, pagelayer_footer_code) settable only by administrators, and (2) per-post meta values (pagelayer_header_code, pagelayer_body_open_code, pagelayer_footer_code) settable by users with edit_post capability. This is an intentional design — it is a 'custom code' injection feature, equivalent to theme header/footer code fields found in many page builders. The risk is limited to privilege escalation: a subscriber or contributor who gains unexpected access to these meta fields could inject code. Under normal WordPress capability assignments, contributors cannot set arbitrary post meta for published posts.
findings.exploitability: Only exploitable if an attacker gains administrator access (options) or gains the ability to write arbitrary post meta (edit_post capability + lack of meta key restriction). This is intentional functionality, not a vulnerability in the traditional sense.
findings.recommendation: Ensure the meta keys (pagelayer_header_code, pagelayer_body_open_code, pagelayer_footer_code) are registered with proper capability checks via register_meta() or protected via a capability gate before saving. Document this as a privileged 'custom code injection' feature in the plugin's security model.

findings.title: Stored XSS via unsanitized no_found echoed in WooCommerce products AJAX handler
findings.description:
The pagelayer_products_ajax handler (wp_ajax only, requires authentication) reads $_POST['atts']['no_found'] directly into $no_found (line 1841) and echoes it unescaped in two places: (1) inside a WooCommerce shortcode no-results closure at line 1877: `echo '
'.$no_found.'
'`, and (2) at line 1893: `$content = '
'. $no_found .'
'`. This handler requires an authenticated WordPress session (no nopriv registration), and access requires a valid pagelayer_ajax nonce (check_ajax_referer). Any authenticated user who can obtain the nonce (i.e., any logged-in user including subscribers on sites that grant all users editor access) could exploit this. However, most WordPress sites restrict pagelayer_ajax nonces to editors/admins. The WooCommerce context is required.
findings.exploitability: If an attacker is an authenticated user with access to the pagelayer_ajax nonce, they can inject arbitrary HTML/JS into the response. The response is echoed directly (no JSON encoding), so stored or reflected XSS in the no-product-found placeholder is achievable. On sites where low-privilege users can interact with WooCommerce page builder widgets, this is medium risk.
findings.recommendation: Sanitize $no_found with wp_kses_post() before echoing, or at minimum with esc_html() if HTML is not intended. Apply this consistently at both echo points (lines 1877 and 1893).

findings.title: file_put_contents to active theme directory - Requires administrator capability
findings.description: The pagelayer_export_template handler writes template files (.pgl extension) and a pagelayer.conf (JSON) file to the active theme directory using file_put_contents(). This handler is protected by check_ajax_referer('pagelayer_builder') and requires current_user_can('edit_theme_options'), which is administrator-level capability. The filenames written are derived from post_name fields of pagelayer templates (sanitized via sanitize_title() for draft names) and the literal 'pagelayer.conf'. There is no arbitrary filename injection from user input for the .pgl files since post_name is trusted database content, but there is no validation that $v->post_name does not contain path traversal sequences (e.g., '../'). The .pgl and .conf files contain serialized template content, not executable PHP.
findings.exploitability: Requires administrator capability. An administrator could use this to write files to the theme directory, but administrators already have direct filesystem access. Path traversal via crafted post_name is theoretically possible but limited in impact since the files are not .php and would not execute as code.
findings.recommendation: Apply basename() to $v->post_name before constructing the file path to prevent any path traversal. Although the current risk is low, defensive path sanitization is best practice.

findings.title: parse_str on unsanitized POST data in widget AJAX handler - Limited impact, auth-gated
findings.description: Two instances of parse_str() on POST data: (1) Line 199 in pagelayer_wp_widget_ajax: parse_str(stripslashes($_POST['values']), $data) — this handler requires current_user_can('edit_theme_options') (admin). The parsed data is used to populate widget instances. (2) Line 2260 in pagelayer_get_post_cats (approximately): parse_str($_POST['new_cat'], $formdata) — the formdata is then passed to wp_insert_category() with the cat_name and pagelayer_cat_parent fields extracted. Neither instance has input sanitization before parse_str(). In case (2), category_name should be sanitized before insertion. The pagelayer_cat_parent is cast to integer in the comparison but not in the actual value passed. Both handlers are auth-gated with nonces and capability checks.
findings.exploitability: Low. Both handlers require authenticated access with elevated capabilities. However, the category insertion case could allow a privileged user to create oddly named categories via URL-encoded bypass, and the widget case could allow manipulation of widget instance data. Real-world impact is minimal given the required authentication level.
findings.recommendation: Sanitize formdata fields individually after parse_str: use sanitize_text_field() on category_name, intval() on pagelayer_cat_parent, and validate/allowlist widget instance keys before passing to widget update methods.

summary: The Pagelayer plugin presents a medium overall risk profile. The most significant confirmed findings are: (1) PL-004 - the pagelayer_infinite_posts nopriv handler passes unsanitized request arrays to the shortcode content renderer, requiring deeper shortcode-level review to determine full XSS/injection impact; (2) PL-008 - a stored/reflected XSS in the WooCommerce product widget AJAX handler where $_POST['atts']['no_found'] is echoed without escaping (requires authenticated access); (3) PL-006 - a reflected XSS in the editor iframe src from unescaped QUERY_STRING, exploitable only against authenticated editor-role users; (4) PL-003 - username enumeration and lack of rate limiting in the login form AJAX handler. Several automated findings were false positives: the exec() alert was a comment, not a function call; the givecss handler correctly whitelists files; the header/footer code echo is intentional admin functionality; and the contact form submission correctly CRLF-strips user data before header interpolation. The file_put_contents and parse_str findings are true positives but low risk due to strong authentication gates.
